The two policies are logically equivalent but compile into different scripts.

The spending cost is also almost the same assuming the two keys are equally likely to be used (with more weight on the script than the spending transaction in the case of the 'thresh' policy). If however key1 is more likely to be used than key2, the 'or' policy is significantly more economical on average.

Therefore the 'or' script policy should be favored in this case, with the most frequent key in front.

Therefore the 'or' script policy should be favored in this case, with the most frequent key in front.
The two policies are logically equivalent and compile into the same script.
The policy with the 'or' and 'and' operators, though it would seem logically equivalent to the 'thresh' policy, actually does not compile into script. Instead it produces an error.
`hash256(H)` applies the SHA-256 hash function twice while `sha256(H)` applies it once. But the spending cost is the same as the on-chain footprint is the same for both operators.

`hash160(H)` applies the SHA-256 hash function followed by the RIPEMD-160 hash function while `ripemd160(H)` only applies the RIPEMD-160 hash function. But the spending cost is the same as the on-chain footprint is the same for both operators.

`hash256(H)` results in a 64-character hexadecimal hash while `hash160(H)` only results in a 40-character hexadecimal hash. Therefore `hash160(H)` has a smaller on-chain footprint and is less costly to spend.

There are two types of absolute timelocks: based on block height and based on block time (given as a unix timestamp). If the value of the timelock is lower than 500000000, it is interpreted as a block height threshold, otherwise it is interpreted as a block time threshold. Using a block time threshold is slightly more expensive as it takes more space on-chain, but the time threshold can be targeted more precisely than with a block height.
